sentences.text: Right.
Yes, she was selling her jewelry one at a time-- that she still had.
And of course, quite a bit we-- she left it with some friends-- non-Jewish friends, and never recuperated.
And-- and she had, I remember, a beautiful ermine shawl, you know, beautiful.
And that was given to someone.
And of course, later on they told me that it's gone.
Somebody stole it.
So-- so what happened, my father got an old trunk and made like a stand out of it.
And my mother, who never worked in her life, and who wasn't a very strong person physically, she was selling frankfurters.
And so when I was in-- in with her, living already in Lvov, I was helping her, too.
And I still can see my mother with those socks.
She couldn't afford to buy gloves, so she had those old knitted socks from-- that the-- the men didn't wear anymore.
They were torn.
And we put them on the hand.
And there was like a little heater with coal.
And sometimes it didn't work out, because in the winter, it was very, very cold.
So the two of us were selling those frankfurters.
So financially, we were a mess.
And in the same town, there was a-- my father's personal lawyer.
And-- and I must say, he wasn't a great Jew-lover, but he was a wonderful human being as far as our family is concerned.
And he always promised my father that if something ever happens to him, he'll take care of the family.
So-- and he did.
So it was very tough financially.
Well-- well, it is my oldest brother who was married and already had a baby.
It was taken away, right away, by the Red Army.
Abrasha.
So he was taken away by the Red Army.
And as I said, his wife and-- came to stay with us.
And my-- my-- oh-- married sister was married to this accountant, and she remained in Ostrog.
And he was making a living with the Russians.
And-- and then my younger brother-- I had two brothers-- and my younger brother-- I don't really what know he was doing.
In the beginning, he wasn't even staying with us in the room.
So I don't know how he was making a living.
I don't remember.
My sister got a job teaching elementary school in the Russian-- because she spoke Russian.
And she didn't need a degree for that at that time.
And-- and I was with my mother.
Well, this was 1940, so it's 17.
Nobody talked about it.
It was just a big tragedy.
Everybody was-- was looking at each other.
And my sister, the one who-- that-- that was teaching, were just wonderful.
Because she was a very personable girl.
So she managed to-- to teach elementary school and also help the Russians in-- in the kitchen sometimes, and got a job there.
So she always managed to bring us some white bread, and white bread with a big thing, you know, and the Russian pumpernickel with the-- with the peasants' bread.
I love pumpernickel more than anything.
And so she managed to bring us some potatoes sometimes.
It-- it was very, very difficult.
It was-- they got-- but somehow, I don't remember any of us crying until the Germany-- I mean, we knew it's bad, but it will be over.
That was our attitude.
So until Germany, somehow we pulled ourselves together.
And it was OK, because we felt everybody else was in the same boat.
No.
I just went-- I went to school.
As I told you, I went to medical school.
And--
In Lvov.
At this time, yeah.
I went.
You did not need-- you had to pass the test in order to get in.
You didn't have to have a high school diploma.
And-- and that was with the Russians.
And you know, we-- there were some kids that-- I don't even know if there were any other Jewish kids.
And then in Warsaw, it was a different-- there.
When I went to Warsaw, and after there, in 1944, when I was already liberated, and-- I became very good friends with a few girls there.
And I suspected that some of them are Jewish.
Nobody ever talked about it, and nobody ever said anything.
But we were five kids, and then we realized that all five of us were Jewish.
And each of us was afraid to admit it, because we were still in fear that maybe the Germans will come back.
And maybe somebody will tell about us.
So-- and somehow, we were attracted to each other.
And then all of a sudden, one of us asked, and we realized that-- I don't remember which one it was that asked, if it was me or somebody else.
It could be me, because I always ask questions.
So and all of a sudden we realized that all five of us-- so we became very, very close.
And-- but in Lvov, I don't-- I don't remember any Jewish kids.
Well, in-- in 1940 were the Russians.
So the Russians, as far as-- Jews could go to-- to Russian schools at that time.
You mean everybody else?
They were Polish kids.
Yeah.
But they didn't look at you as a Jew.
They just--
No, because obviously, they didn't recognize.
Because I survived as a-- as a Polish girl.
Because-- and I was surrounded by Poles.
So obviously, nobody thought it that there might be a Jewish girl here.
So they didn't suspect it.
And I didn't tell them, no.
And you're scared of your own shadow already.
Yeah, because first of all, the-- the Russians weren't exactly a bowl of cherries either.
They-- they weren't big Jew-lovers themselves.
And-- and certainly, they committed a lot of atrocities.
So the best thing is not to say anything.
Oh, yeah.
The Russians-- even in peacetime-- I mean, they were in-- if they didn't like you-- if you said one bad word about Stalin, and this is it.
And you know very well what happened in 1937.
Stalin decided that all the Jewish doctors are against him, and there was a tremendous-- tremendous persecution at that time.
And a lot of them were sent to-- to camps and never came back.
So it'd be Stalin himself.
Because he-- right.
Land law-- a land owner was the biggest enemy.
Because this is a man who is-- who is torturing peasants.
I mean, he is-- he was taking advantage of them.
They-- they worked very hard, but they didn't even make-- the peasants didn't even expect to work less.
Because to them, it was very important to have some kind of income.
And they-- they were brought up that way.
And no-- no peasant at that time even fought against the capitalists.
To them, this was a fact of life.

And I always, when I talk to my own husband, I always tell him that when I think about my past, I think about this great playwright, Chekhov, who said that it's always-- you are telling me that you want to improve the lots of peasants, but you-- you form your own committees.
You make your decisions based on what you want them to be in.

But-- but if you really want to help peasants, and you make them work 6 hours instead of 12 hours, and before they get exhausted and tired from bending over, and so then I can see that you really want to do something.
But you're just saying that you want to do something for the peasants.
So there's no doubt about it.
Peasants had a very, very hard life and lived in very poor conditions.
My father wasn't already with-- and you're talking about in '39?
Until--
Yeah, yeah.
Well, so until '41, the Pole who rented a room to us, knew that we were Jewish.
He knew.
And he resented the fact that my sister, every once in a while, would bring a Russian soldier-- Russian officer-- to the house, because they were very nice to her.
So she would invite them.
And so he felt that we are communists.
And he, of course, didn't like that.
They was frightened of-- of the Russians-- the Poles.
And obviously, he didn't like them.
So-- so they, of course, knew.
Because we went there.
And-- but otherwise , we did not hide our identity.
No, used the-- the regular name-- Eisenberg.
When the Russians came-- you mean when the Germans --
Yeah.
The first day--
That was in September--
--in September.
Right, yeah-- September '41.
But I thought I have what the day.
The-- the Germans came out with an appeal to the Jewish community that they want to-- they need money.
And it was like $3 million-- something of that sort.
And if the town-- the city of Lvov is going to give 10 hostages within a day, they will-- they will release the hostages if the money is given to them.
They had 24 hours to submit the money.
This was Jewish hostages, or--
The-- the Jewish hostages.
They will take 10 Jewish hostages.
And-- and they came to us, the Gestapo.
Three Gestapo officers came to us.
And they said that they would like to take my father as a hostage, but he shouldn't worry about, because he will go to a camp and work the few days.
And then the money will be given.
He will be released.
So my father asked them-- so my father asked them, could his son come along with him?
And so they started to look each other, and said they have to have a conference, the three of them-- handsome Germans, you know, and beautifully dressed.
You know, they were always immaculate.
And they had a short conference, and they said, it's OK.
They will take him.
Now did your family trust the Germans?
Well, obviously, it was the biggest mistake of his life.
This was his beloved son, I must tell you, that he really was very, very close to-- to my-- my brother.
And my brother didn't want to go.
And he says, why don't you go, and I'll see what happens.
And he says, I want you to come with me.
And my brother said, well, if you want me to come, I will go.
That was the younger one, Grisza.
And meanwhile, my father-- so they were going to pick him up in the morning-- and to be ready.
You know, when my father got in touch with my-- the lawyer, that I told you of-- the non-Jewish lawyer, and the lawyer said to him, don't go.
He says, what do you mean?
I-- I said I'll work.
He says, don't go.
I'll take you away from here.
He said, but I already said Grisza is coming too.
He said, you made a big mistake.
He says, I am telling you.
I'll pick you up at night.
And then he said, what will happen to my family?
And he says, what will happen to all of them?
He says, I'll take care of them.
He said, but they will not let you.
Before anything will happen, they'll come and take them away.
And I'll tell you one thing.
He didn't listen to the lawyer.
And in the morning, he got up.
My father was 41 years old, and he was all gray.
Until-- and if someone would attest, it's impossible.
I'm telling you, it's a fact.
He was all-- he had maybe one or two gray hair.
He was all gray.
And they came.
And they took the two of them.
And the room where we lived was like on a little hill.
So we were all watching, because a truck was waiting for them.
And my sister, the one who is teaching, was following the truck.
And about 10 minutes later, she came, and we heard the screams already from a big distance-- that she heard 10 shots.
They arrived there.
So we lost right away the-- my brother and my-- and my father.
They were shot immediately.
So, I will never forget, when she came in, and before she even started to say what happened, she was hysterical.
And all the Poles, she told us, were looking out from the windows to see what was happening.
And I will never forget how my mother was controlling herself and just hugging us.
You have to be calm.
You have to be calm-- the one that she was really worried very much what will happen.
I always remember that scene.
And then of course, she went crazy herself, my mother.
So-- so this was the first thing.
Then--
The what?
Well, it's certain-- and I'll tell you one thing.
In a case like this, somehow, no matter how bad it is, you gain strength.
You somehow-- you say to yourself, you must go on.
You can't let it get to you.
And we started to-- I still remember the scene in the corner of the room, how we started to wipe our-- the tears, and started to console mother.
She shouldn't worry.
And so the lawyer came.
And-- and he saw that things are getting very bad.
And they started to talk about organizing a ghetto in Lvov.
My sis--
Yeah.
--who worked-- taught in the school--
And--
My sister-in-law.
Her child.
And my oldest brother was taken to the Red Army and was very concerned about his wife.
So he wanted to come back to escape.
And they told him, don't run away, because the-- the Germans are coming, and they will kill you.
And he said, I don't care.
I have to try.
I can't leave her alone.
And they did kill him.
A friend of his saw-- saw it and told it.
So my--
Yeah.

While my brother-in-law, who remained in Austria with my sister and their two children, a lot of people started to run away to Russia, because they already heard what is happening, that the Germans are coming.
And already, other countries were taken over, concentration camps are built.
So they started to run away to Russia.
Some of them ran alone, leaving their children and wives with this-- that soon the war will be over, and they'll come back.
Some of them took their family.
And according to some friends, my sister was begging him to take her and the children along.
And he said, look, I cannot do it.
It's-- look what is going on here in the mobs.
You will be all right here.
And I'll come back.
And she was running after him, and he just left.
So my sister remained penniless and with the two little children.
And she and an uncle of mine took-- took her reluctantly to his house in another town, not far from Ostrog, and said he'll keep her for awhile.
Then later on, she became a burden, because he didn't have enough food for his own family.
And then when the Germans came in to their town, they right away-- a massacre began.
And she started-- everybody started to run to the forest to hide.
And she ran.
One was five, and the other one was three.
So she kept one in the arms and one she was carrying.
And as she was approaching the edge of the forest, there were three shots.
And they were right away killed.
People who saw it, who survived.
One or two survived.
I never knew before.
A lot of things I learned after the war.
So this was the end of-- of them too.
So-- and of course, she had already a very hard life before, because they threw her out of the-- she couldn't be in the house.
Our house became the city hall.
And then-- and then as I told you, my friend even got married there, got her license there.
And they started to redo the whole thing, because the house was a beautiful house.
As a matter of fact, they were really ahead of their time, because I didn't see this kind of home before.
I understand that when they were building the house, they had painters coming from bigger cities.
And-- and let's say the living room had all kinds of flowers painted by real paint-- by artists, in the living room.
And between the living room and my father's office was like a glass wall that you could open up to make one big-- big room, and they all close it and have two separate big rooms.
And each of them had a magnificent, marble fireplace.
And it was very, very beautiful.
And my sister and I had the bedroom that was in the middle of the house with a skylight.
It'd be the whole room.
The ceiling was like a skylight.
And so that-- and we had a big table in the middle of the room that we had a lot of friends talking, always sitting, shouting around this table.
And everybody, of course, had a bedroom.
And it had a big, big kitchen, two dining rooms.
It had three entrances.
And the-- and the-- next to the kitchen was a 'sikka'-- sukkah built-- with this, that the ceiling was a removable ceiling.
So you open it up.
And then when you open it up, you saw a lot of greenery on there.
So-- and so-- and then, of course, with the winter, it was closed.
And we were all sitting.
And there was a small sukkah.
But I still remember, we were all sitting along this long table.
It was very informal, very, like, rustic.
And the-- the windows of the kitchen were looking out to the backyard.
And there you had-- we had to have the stable, because my father would always come with three horses home.
So you had a stable for the horses, and you had for-- we had a cow, one cow there, that gave us always milk.
And then we had, in the backyard, a big like a-- a bungalow.
And there were no refrigerators, but the whole floor had blocks of ice.
So a lot of our neighbors would come and keep their-- their food there, especially for the holiday.
They would-- they would buy a lot of stuff, and they didn't have any-- any ice.
So I will always remember they would come and ask for permission.
My mother said, you don't have to ask permission.
You just go and put it in.
So another way in-- in the house had two big gates, the-- the yard, the front yard.
So one was for the horses to come in, and the other side was to come out.
And-- and my mother-- my maternal grandmother lived with-- in downstairs in our house.
So-- and she had a-- a-- a daughter who was not married.
So she was always sitting in front of the gate, right inside.
And a passerby would at least stop and chat with her, because she was in a wheelchair and couldn't walk around.
My mother spent every day some time with her.
And we also had a-- a big, big foyer.
We had three entrances.
So it was-- one was a formal, and one was coming in to-- to a foyer.
Another one was coming through a little hole to the kitchen.
So it was very, very nice.
And my-- my grandmother lived downstairs.
They had an apartment for her and her daughter.
And then there was another apartment for another aunt, my mother's sister, who had a family with three children.
They had an apartment.
And then there was a little apartment for the servants who lived downstairs-- and a big laundry also downstairs.
And anytime my father would come for Friday-- usually it would be Friday-- my mother would be dressed in her best clothes.
And-- and I remember always she had wavy hair, always her hair washed.
And we always teased her.
Aha, father is coming.
All week you are sick.
You're not feeling well.
Now you're feeling wonderful, and you're dressed up.
She said that's how you have to be when you're expecting your husband.
I remember that she always catered to my husband.
Yiddish, and Polish, and Russian.
My father's secretary was Russian.
So because he was from the intelligentsia, and he didn't like the Russian.
He was really very aristocratic in Russian.
And most of our peasant servants were Ukrainian.
So you really had to know Russian Ukrainian.
And of course, we-- my parents and my grandparents spoke a lot of Yiddish, too.
So it was really fun.
And any time my father was coming, we heard already the horse or the trot of the horses.
We all ran out.
And so it was really a very-- it was a beautiful childhood.
And I'll tell you, a lot of things I don't even remember.
My-- my friends who survived-- the few friends who survived-- remind me of things about my parents and about my family, which really, I'm delighted to hear.
I told you, I went two years ago for the first time back to Warsaw.
And just to tell you one thing.
And I met there, a girl that went to school, to medical school in Lvov with me.
And she was telling me wonderful stories about my-- about my family that I never knew, yes.
Was I in medical school?
No.
No, because in-- in-- I never saw the Germans again.
Because you're talking about in-- between '41 and '43?
Yeah.
Yeah.
OK, at that time, you-- of course, you had-- you could work.
They needed the Jews for work, and that's the reason some ghettos lasted much longer.
Let's say they had factories of making paper out of clothes.
So they needed people to-- to take apart all the clothes.
And let's say the clothes that we were taking apart came from-- from dead Jews already.
And I'll tell you in a minute how we knew.
So anyway, there were all kinds of jobs that they needed-- the Germans.
So-- and they need workers.
So if you knew someone-- and my brother's friend happened to be on the city council, on the Judenrat.
And-- This is in Lvov?
In Lvov.
And he gave us permits.
And he gave us permits for working.
So not everybody could get it.
So it was for my sister, my sister-in-law, and myself.
Since we were working, and there was a baby to take care of, they told us that my mother would be protected, that she can stay home and take care of the baby.
So-- so that's what's happened.
We were working, and as we were taking apart, it was heartbreaking.
We found all kinds of little notes saying goodbye to certain people.
Some-- some-- some jackets had blood all over.
Some said-- it told us very briefly, I-- I am getting-- I'll be killed in a minute, and I'll never see you.
So we started to realize what is happening, what-- what is going on.
Pardon?
Well, it wasn't certainly easy.
But anyway, so then--
No, no.
But then one day-- [SIGHS]
They would announce officially on the radio that today, Jews between the age of 17 and 25, please be sure to stay outside.
You'll be picked up for work.
Then they will say, women from 40 to 45 we need for a certain job.
And they never came back.
But of course, everybody was so preoccupied with yourself.
So then one day, when they came with an announcement and said, women-- my mother was about 42-- women will be taken from the age of 40 to 50, whatever it is, will be taken to work.
And my sister-- we were working together, and this was quite close to where we lived.
I didn't tell you yet where we were living.
So my sister started to run home and find out where Mother is.
